definition: Astrology is divination by the stars and planets — reading celestial positions for their influence on earthly life. For most of history it was inseparable from astronomy.
history: Greek *astron*, 'star,' + *-logia*, 'study.' From Babylonian omen-astronomy through Ptolemy's *Tetrabiblos* (2nd c.) and the great Arabic astrologers, astrology was a rigorous technical science — it chose the hour to make a talisman or crown a king. Only in the 17th century did astrology and astronomy divorce; Kepler cast horoscopes to the end. RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues its apparatus of aspects, dignities, and houses.

def_sources.label: Tamsyn Barton, Ancient Astrology
def_sources.abstract: This book explains how astrology (the belief that the positions of stars and planets influence human events) developed in Mesopotamia and spread through Greece and Rome, and examines the social role astrologers played and why the practice appealed to people at every level of ancient society.

commentary: Astrology sits at the center of the field's biggest methodological quarrel: whether it belongs to the history of *science* or the history of *superstition*. Lynn Thorndike's monumental *History of Magic and Experimental Science* refused the tidy story of reason shedding astral nonsense, treating prediction and natural inquiry as intertwined. Later scholars — Nicolas Weill-Parot, David Pingree, and Paola Zambelli — sharpened the distinctions Thorndike blurred, separating astral image theory, mathematical practice, and judicial prognostication. The field now stresses that astrology was a rigorous technical discipline whose divorce from astronomy was late and contingent, not a failure waiting to be corrected.

commentary_sources.label: Lynn Thorndike, A History of Magic and Experimental Science
commentary_sources.abstract: This massive eight-volume history, published between 1923 and 1958, traces how magic and science developed together in Europe from the early Christian centuries through the 1600s. Thorndike's central argument is that early scientific thinking did not spring up in opposition to magic and the occult, but actually grew out of the same beliefs and practices.

commentary_sources.label: Nicolas Weill-Parot, on astrology and occult knowledge
commentary_sources.abstract: This detailed study (published as 'Les images astrologiques au Moyen Age et à la Renaissance') traces how medieval and Renaissance scholars tried to justify 'astrological images' — talismans believed to draw power down from the stars — as a form of natural science rather than forbidden magic. It shows how this argument shaped bigger debates about nature, technology, and the line between science and the occult.

commentary_sources.label: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic from the Medieval Latin and Islamic World to Renaissance Europe
commentary_sources.abstract: A collection of the author's essays tracing how astrology (reading the stars to predict events) passed from Islamic and Latin medieval scholars into the Renaissance, and how astrologers actually worked as court advisors and public intellectuals, not just armchair theorists.

etymology.note: Astrology and astronomy were a single science until the 17th century pried them apart; Kepler cast horoscopes for a living.
etymology.hook: When astrology and astronomy filed for divorce.

flavor_snippets.html:
"Well, that's another night of research spoiled."
—Claire, batfolk <mark>astrologer</mark>
